reasoning_model_1: The user requested a 'smack of the lips' corresponding to the text 'Tsk'. Model 1 successfully interprets the text 'Tsk' as a non-linguistic vocalization rather than a word. The audio (represented as 't... t...') contains the requested dental clicks or lip sounds at the beginning (0.0s-0.5s), effectively conveying the 'Tsk' sound. The subsequent speech 'That was a truly satisfying meal today' is delivered clearly. The model correctly handles the controllable feature by producing the sound effect.
reasoning_model_2: Model 2 fails to interpret the 'Tsk' as a non-linguistic marker. Instead, it phonetically reads the text as the word 'Tisk' (0.0s-0.5s). This violates the instruction to produce a 'smack of the lips' or the associated sound effect, treating the onomatopoeia as linguistic content. Consequently, it fails to generate the required paralinguistic feature.
model_comparison: Model 1 is the clear winner as it correctly identifies and generates the Non-Linguistic Vocalization ('Tsk' sound/clicks) requested by the user. Model 2 fails completely in this dimension by reading the onomatopoeia as a spoken word ('Tisk'), which destroys the intended effect and ignores the 'smack of the lips' instruction. Model 1 demonstrates superior control over paralinguistic features.
